flashcards.Back: A logical grouping of network devices that behave as if they are connected to the same wire, regardless of their physical location. It is used to segment broadcast domains.
A switch port configured to carry traffic for only one specific VLAN. it is typically used to connect end-user devices like PCs and printers.
An access port that has been manually assigned to a specific VLAN ID by a network administrator.
A VLAN configured to carry user-generated traffic, such as email, web browsing, and file transfers, isolated from management or voice traffic.
A specialized VLAN configuration on an access port that allows an IP phone to send tagged voice traffic while a connected PC sends untagged data traffic.
VLAN IDs from 1 to 1005. These are used in small-to-medium enterprise networks and are stored in the vlan.dat file.
VLAN IDs from 1006 to 4094. These are primarily used by service providers and are stored in the switch's running configuration.
A 12-bit field in an Ethernet header tag that identifies the specific VLAN to which a frame belongs, supporting up to 4096 unique IDs.
The Cisco IOS command used to force an interface to function as a permanent access port, disabling DTP negotiation.
A configuration state where a VLAN is defined in the database but is administratively disabled, preventing any traffic from being forwarded within that VLAN.
The logical area in a network where a broadcast frame is forwarded. Creating VLANs effectively divides one large broadcast domain into multiple smaller ones.
The binary file stored in a switch's flash memory that contains the configuration for all normal range VLANs.
A Cisco-proprietary protocol that automates the addition, deletion, and renaming of VLANs across multiple synchronized switches in a domain.
A VTP mode where a switch does not synchronize its VLAN database with other switches but still forwards VTP advertisements received from its neighbors.
The ability for hosts in different VLANs to communicate with each other, which requires a Layer 3 device like a router or a multilayer switch.
The process of carrying traffic from multiple VLANs over a single physical link between two switches or a switch and a router.
A switch interface configured to support multiple VLANs simultaneously using a tagging protocol like 802.1Q.
The industry-standard trunking protocol that inserts a 4-byte VLAN tag into the original Ethernet frame header.
The specific VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk that carries all untagged traffic. For security, both ends of a trunk must be configured with the same native VLAN ID.
The pre-configured VLAN on a Cisco switch (VLAN 1) to which all ports are assigned out of the box.
A Cisco-proprietary protocol used to automatically negotiate whether a link should become a trunk or an access port between two connected switches.
The configured DTP setting on a port (e.g., dynamic auto, dynamic desirable), which determines how the port attempts to negotiate trunking.
The actual resulting state of a port (either 'trunking' or 'static access') after DTP negotiation with its neighbor has completed.
A DTP mode where the port waits passively to be asked to become a trunk. it will only trunk if the neighbor is set to 'desirable' or 'on'.
A DTP mode where the port actively attempts to negotiate a trunk link with its neighbor. it is the most aggressive DTP setting.
A command that prevents a port from sending DTP frames. it is used when a trunk is manually configured ('mode trunk') to improve security and efficiency.
A security configuration on a trunk port that specifies which VLAN IDs are permitted to send and receive traffic across the link.
A technique used to restrict the transmission of broadcast and multicast traffic on a trunk to only the VLANs that are actually present on the remote switch.
A malicious attack where a device sends frames with multiple VLAN tags to gain unauthorized access to a different VLAN without passing through a router.
An error condition where two ends of a trunk have different native VLAN IDs, potentially leading to traffic leaking between VLANs.
